source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5178__New-Mexico-Public-Regulation-Commission/2025.11.20 New Mexico Public Regulation Commission Comment -- RM 26-4 .txt
overall_summary: New Mexico PRC supports transparent and standardized interconnection procedures but emphasizes that FERC must respect state regulatory authority over retail rates, resource planning, and cost allocation. The commission urges a cooperative federalism approach that preserves state jurisdiction and proposes raising the 20 MW threshold to 50 MW.

quotes.text: The NMPRC supports the Commission's efforts to provide transparent, standardized, and efficient interconnection procedures for large loads and hybrid facilities on the transmission system.

the NMPRC seeks to ensure that the rulemaking adequately accounts for coordination with state regulatory processes and considers direct and indirect impacts to the NMPRC's authority over retail rates and cost recovery from retail customers in New Mexico.
quotes.concern: requires state coordination on retail cost impacts

quotes.text: FERC has consistently contended that the Federal Power Act creates two separate, exclusive spheres of authority: FERC regulates wholesale sales of electricity and interstate transmission, while states retain exclusive control over retail sales, retail rate design, and the siting and operation of generation and distribution facilities.
quotes.concern: FPA allocation of wholesale vs retail authority

quotes.text: states retain exclusive authority over retail sales, including retail rate design and the allocation of costs among retail customers. States alone determine which entities may sell power at retail and the rates, terms, and conditions of those retail sales.
quotes.concern: state control over retail rates and cost allocation

quotes.text: FERC cannot require New Mexico ratepayers to finance new generation or infrastructure that has not gone through New Mexico's planning and approval requirements, whether initiated through the IRP process, certificate proceedings, or any of the other regulatory pathways the NMPRC uses to review and approve new resources.
quotes.concern: large load costs must pass state review before ratepayer financing

quotes.text: if FERC assumes jurisdiction over approving new large loads, those interconnections could be approved without any proceeding to evaluate their associated costs, depriving the NMPRC of its ability to regulate interconnecting customers and determine how associated generation and transmission costs should be allocated to New Mexico ratepayers.
quotes.concern: federal approval without cost evaluation removes state cost-allocation control

quotes.text: protection of state jurisdiction is essential to preventing retail customers from subsidizing large-load customers or bearing unplanned risks. FERC should ensure that any final rule respects the FPA's jurisdictional boundary and preserves New Mexico's sovereign authority to regulate retail service, resource decisions, and economic development consistent with state law and legislative priorities.
quotes.concern: state jurisdiction protects retail customers from cost-shifting

quotes.text: FERC should approach this rulemaking through a model of cooperative federalism, which the United States Supreme Court has described as a program that allows the States, within limits established by federal minimum standards, to enact and administer their own regulatory programs, structured to meet their own particular needs.

Regarding the proposed minimum of 20 MW, this threshold may be too low at this time of an extremely large number of load requests. FERC should consider a larger threshold, and should consider if this threshold compliments, or conflicts with, similar initiatives being pursued by RTOs. The NMPRC suggests starting with a minimum threshold of 50 MW.
any approval process for hybrid facilities should allow states the same discretion they currently have over new generation.
quotes.concern: hybrid facilities require state approval discretion

bins.name: State regulatory authority over retail
bins.description: NMPRC asserts that states retain exclusive control over retail rate design, cost allocation, and resource planning under the Federal Power Act. Large load interconnections must pass state review processes to protect ratepayers from unplanned costs.

bins.name: Raise large load threshold
bins.description: The 20 MW threshold proposed in the ANOPR may be too low given the current surge in load requests. NMPRC recommends starting with a 50 MW minimum and examining whether it aligns with RTO initiatives.

bins.name: Cost allocation through state processes
bins.description: NMPRC insists that generation and transmission costs associated with large loads must be evaluated and allocated through state planning and approval pathways. Federal approval without cost review would deprive the state of cost-allocation authority and risk shifting costs to retail customers.

bins.name: Support transparent procedures
bins.description: NMPRC supports FERC's efforts to create transparent, standardized, and efficient interconnection procedures for large loads and hybrid facilities.

bins.name: Protect local retail customers
bins.description: State jurisdiction is essential to prevent retail customers from subsidizing large-load customers or bearing unplanned risks. Rules must respect local conditions and priorities.

bins.name: State discretion over hybrid facilities
bins.description: Hybrid facilities approval processes should grant states the same discretion they have over new generation resources.

bins.name: Ratepayer financing safeguards
bins.description: NMPRC emphasizes that ratepayers cannot be required to finance new generation or infrastructure without state review of costs and necessity.

bins.name: Cooperative federalism framework
bins.description: FERC should adopt a cooperative federalism model that allows states to administer their own regulatory programs within federal minimum standards, respecting local conditions and state policy priorities.
